name: Delivery System
overview:
A delivery system connects customers placing orders with couriers who fulfill them. The system must match
orders to available couriers in real time, track courier locations continuously, provide live order status updates to
customers, and optimize routing to minimize delivery time. The system handles high concurrency during peak
hours (lunch/dinner rushes), must be fault-tolerant to courier disconnections, and needs to handle geographic
distribution across many cities.

inScope: Live order status updates pushed to customer

items: Support 1M concurrent orders and 500k active couriers
99.9% availability; peak-hour traffic spikes (lunch/dinner) must not degrade matching
Eventual consistency for courier locations; strong consistency for order assignment (no double-assignment)
ETA recalculation every 30 seconds with <200ms computation latency

entities.name: Order
entities.schema:
{
  order_id: UUID,
  customer_id: string,
  merchant_id: string,

  courier_id: string (nullable),
  status: enum(PLACED|MATCHED|PICKED_UP|IN_TRANSIT|DELIVERED|CANCELLED),
  pickup_location: {lat, lng},
  dropoff_location: {lat, lng},
  placed_at: timestamp,
  estimated_delivery: timestamp
}

entities.name: Courier
entities.schema:
{
  courier_id: string,
  status: enum(AVAILABLE|BUSY|OFFLINE),
  current_location: {lat, lng},
  last_seen: timestamp,
  active_order_id: string (nullable)
}

approaches.title: PostgreSQL with PostGIS
approaches.description: Store courier locations in a PostgreSQL table with PostGIS geometry columns. PostGIS supports geospatial queries (find couriers within radius) but at 500k couriers updating every 5 seconds = 100k writes/sec, PostgreSQL cannot sustain this write load.
approaches.tradeoff: Write throughput too high for relational DB; PostGIS queries slow at scale.

approaches.title: Redis Geospatial (GEOADD/GEORADIUS)
approaches.description: Use Redis GEOADD to store courier locations as geospatial members. GEORADIUS queries find all couriers within a given radius in O(N+log(M)) time. Redis handles 100k writes/sec easily. Data is in-memory so reads are sub-millisecond. Persistence via Redis AOF/RDB for recovery after restart.
approaches.tradeoff: Redis memory cost for 500k couriers; single Redis instance is a bottleneck.

approaches.title: Redis Geo + Geohash Sharding
approaches.description: Shard Redis by geohash prefix (first 4 characters = ~40km cells). Each shard handles a geographic region. Courier location updates go to the shard for their current geohash. Matching queries fan out to nearby geohash shards. Location history is written asynchronously to Cassandra for analytics and dispute resolution.
approaches.tradeoff: Cross-shard queries for couriers near cell boundaries require fan-out; slight complexity.

approaches.title: Polling-Based Matching
approaches.description: A cron job runs every 10 seconds, queries unmatched orders, finds nearby available couriers, and assigns the closest one. Simple but 10-second polling delay is unacceptable. Race conditions when multiple orders compete for the same courier.
approaches.tradeoff: High latency; race conditions; not scalable.

approaches.title: Event-Driven Matching Service
approaches.description: When an order is placed, publish an event to Kafka. A matching service consumes the event, queries Redis for nearby couriers, and assigns the best match using a scoring function (distance + courier rating). Optimistic locking on courier status prevents double-assignment.
approaches.tradeoff: Matching service can become a bottleneck; requires careful concurrency control.

approaches.title: Matching Service with Distributed Lock
approaches.description: Same event-driven approach but courier assignment uses a Redis distributed lock (SETNX with TTL) to prevent race conditions. If no courier is found within 30 seconds, the order is re-queued with an expanded search radius. Matching score = 0.6 × (1/distance) + 0.3 × courier_rating + 0.1 × (1/active_orders).
approaches.tradeoff: Redis lock adds latency; lock TTL must be tuned carefully to avoid deadlocks.

components.name: Order Service
components.responsibility: Creates orders, validates, publishes to Kafka; manages order state machine

components.name: Courier Service
components.responsibility: Receives location heartbeats; updates Redis geo index; manages courier status

components.name: Matching Service
components.responsibility: Consumes order events from Kafka; queries Redis for nearby couriers; assigns with distributed lock

components.name: Tracking Service
components.responsibility: Aggregates courier location updates; pushes live updates to customers via WebSocket

components.name: WebSocket Gateway
components.responsibility: Maintains persistent connections with customers; receives push notifications from Tracking Service

components.name: Redis Geo
components.responsibility: Stores courier locations sharded by geohash; supports radius queries

components.name: Kafka
components.responsibility: Event bus for order lifecycle events and location updates

components.name: PostgreSQL
components.responsibility: Persistent store for orders, couriers, and assignment history

components.name: Cassandra
components.responsibility: Time-series store for courier location history and analytics

components.name: ETA Service
components.responsibility: Computes delivery ETAs using road network graph (OSRM/Google Maps

questions.title: 2. WebSocket Connection Management
questions.content:
1M concurrent orders means up to 1M WebSocket connections. WebSocket gateways are stateful — each
gateway maintains a map of order_id → connection. When the Tracking Service has a location update, it
publishes to a Kafka topic partitioned by order_id. Each WebSocket gateway subscribes to the partitions for its
assigned orders. This ensures each update is delivered to exactly one gateway, which then pushes to the
customer.

questions.title: 3. ETA Calculation
questions.content:
ETA = time_to_pickup + pickup_dwell_time + time_to_dropoff. Time calculations use OSRM (Open Source
Routing Machine) with a pre-loaded road network graph. OSRM computes driving time between two
coordinates in <10ms. ETAs are recalculated every 30 seconds as the courier moves and traffic conditions
change. For cities without OSRM coverage, we fall back to straight-line distance × 1.4 (empirical detour factor)
÷ average_speed.

questions.title: 4. Handling Courier Disconnections
questions.content:
If a courier's last_seen timestamp is >60 seconds old, the system marks them as OFFLINE and re-queues their
active order for re-matching. A dead-letter queue in Kafka holds orders that have failed matching 3 times,
triggering an alert for manual intervention. Couriers reconnecting within 5 minutes can reclaim their active order
if it hasn't been re-matched.

questions.title: 5. Geographic Sharding
questions.content:
The system is deployed per city/region. Each city has its own Redis cluster, Kafka cluster, and matching service
instances. A global routing layer (based on customer/courier IP geolocation) directs traffic to the correct
regional deployment. Cross-city orders (rare) are handled by a global matching service that queries multiple
regional Redis clusters.

keyFlows: ① Customer places order → Order API → Kafka → Matching Service
② Matching: GEORADIUS on Redis Geo → assign courier (Redis lock)
③ Courier sends location every 5s → Courier Svc → Redis Geo
④ Tracking Svc aggregates locations → WebSocket GW → Customer push
⑤ ETA Service recalculates every 30s using OSRM routing

whatToStudyFurther.content:
Expert Note: Study geohash encoding: how it maps 2D coordinates to a 1D string, the precision levels
(1-12 characters), and how neighboring cells share geohash prefixes for efficient proximity queries.
Expert Note: Learn about WebSocket scaling patterns: sticky sessions vs. pub/sub fan-out, how to handle
connection state across multiple gateway instances, and graceful reconnection with exponential backoff.
Expert Note: Understand distributed locking with Redis: the SETNX + EXPIRE pattern, the Redlock
algorithm for multi-node safety, and why lock TTL must exceed the critical section duration.
Expert Note: Study OSRM (Open Source Routing Machine): how it pre-processes OpenStreetMap data
into a contraction hierarchy, enabling sub-millisecond shortest-path queries.
Expert Note: Learn about the CAP theorem trade-offs in location tracking: why eventual consistency is
acceptable for courier positions but strong consistency is required for order assignment.
